name: Science Education 11–12: Curriculum, Pedagogy and Inclusive Practice
week0Message: Have a look around at the first few tiles below — we'll get started next week!

learningOutcomes.title: Science Content & Laboratory Practice
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the concepts, substance and structure of the content and teaching strategies of the 7–10 science syllabus by incorporating practical activities into the laboratory so that students are actively engaged in learning.

learningOutcomes.title: Curriculum Sequencing & Pedagogy
learningOutcomes.description: Organise content into an effective learning and teaching sequence within the context of the 7–10 syllabus in logical, sequential and structured ways utilising appropriate pedagogy to address student learning outcomes.

learningOutcomes.title: Lesson Planning & Conceptual Development
learningOutcomes.description: Plan lesson sequences using knowledge of student learning, content and effective teaching strategies by recognising links in teaching sequences which will develop conceptual understanding and scientific skills and clearly articulate the learning goals.

learningOutcomes.title: Assessment Design & Program Evaluation
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate broad knowledge of strategies that can be used to evaluate teaching programs to improve student learning by planning and documenting an assessment task which includes a range of embedded strategies for assessing student achievement and appropriately mapping outcomes to these strategies.

learningOutcomes.title: Diversity & Responsive Teaching
learningOutcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of teaching strategies, including literacy and numeracy development, that are responsive to the learning strengths and needs of students from diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic backgrounds.

learningOutcomes.title: Professional Learning & Community
learningOutcomes.description: Understand the rationale for continued professional learning, and relevant sources of professional learning including external professionals and community representatives, that can be used to improve personal development and student learning.

assessmentTasks.title: Design and Pedagogy — Practical Science Lessons
assessmentTasks.rationale: Secondary school science teachers need to recognise opportunities for practicals as often as possible. The syllabus mandates a minimum amount of practical work, which can always be exceeded where appropriate. It is essential that teachers can provide instruction and practical opportunities so that students can build their own understanding, working with what they already know and can do. This constructivist approach to science teaching is essential to learning success.
assessmentTasks.aim: The aims of this assessment task are for you to demonstrate: your understanding of syllabus content; your ability to interpret syllabus documents; your capacity to make adjustments to support the learning needs of a given cohort; and how you would integrate a range of practicals as a matter of course in the context of lessons in Years 7–10 science.

parts.title: Practical Science Learning Sequence
parts.description: Design a table of 8–10 practicals in a logical learning sequence for a Knowledge and Understanding strand of your choice.
requirements: Chronological number
Title
Description (20 words max)
NSW syllabus outcome codes
Prior knowledge and skills
Safety considerations
Adjustments for the given cohort

parts.title: Justification
parts.description: Citing scholarly and/or syllabus sources, justify the decisions made in Part A. Address all five required elements.
requirements: Your pedagogical approach and why you chose it
How the sequence supports the given cohort specifically
Relevant syllabus elements — Working Scientifically, General Capabilities, Cross Curriculum Priorities
Your prior knowledge decisions
Your safety decisions
Explain why each decision serves the cohort — not just what you decided

parts.title: Reflection
parts.description: Reflect on what was new to you in this task versus what you already knew well.
requirements: Name one specific professional learning source — not a category, but a specific resource, association, or person
Explain why it is relevant to your strand and your teaching context

criteria.title: Syllabus outcomes, content, and skills
criteria.mustShow: For each practical: the correct NSW Science outcome code (e.g. SC4-WS-01), matched to what the practical actually involves. Include both Knowledge & Understanding and Working Scientifically outcomes. Add General Capabilities or Cross Curriculum Priorities where they genuinely apply.

criteria.title: Sequence — the order of your practicals
criteria.mustShow: A clear, defensible order where earlier practicals introduce the concepts or skills that later ones require. The progression makes sense to a student working through it from the start.

criteria.title: Pedagogical approach and prior knowledge
criteria.mustShow: A named constructivist model (e.g. 5E, inquiry cycle) that is recognisable in how the practicals are sequenced. Prior knowledge that is specific and accurate for each practical — not a generic entry like 'basic science knowledge'.

criteria.title: Safety and inclusion for the given cohort
criteria.mustShow: Safety requirements that are specific to each practical (not the same statement copied across every row). Practical, classroom-ready adjustments for all four groups in the given cohort: EAL/D (2 students), advanced (2 students), behind (3 students), Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (5 students with community connections).

criteria.title: Justification
criteria.mustShow: Scholarly and/or syllabus sources cited for every claim. All five elements addressed: (1) pedagogical approach and why chosen; (2) how sequence supports the given cohort specifically; (3) relevant syllabus elements; (4) prior knowledge decisions; (5) safety decisions. Explain why each decision serves the cohort — not just what was decided.

criteria.title: Professional reflection and learning
criteria.mustShow: Genuine reflection on what was new versus already known. A named, specific professional learning source — not a category like 'NESA resources', but a specific resource, association, or person by name. A justification explaining: why this source, for this strand, for your teaching context.

HD.descriptor: Outcome codes are correct throughout. Every practical is matched to specific content statements. Working Scientifically outcomes, General Capabilities, and Cross Curriculum Priorities appear where they are genuinely relevant — not added to every row by default.

D.descriptor: Outcome codes are correct. Each practical is matched to relevant content statements. Working Scientifically outcomes are included for most practicals.

C.descriptor: Outcome codes are mostly correct with minor errors. Practicals are matched to content statements, though some matches are imprecise. Working Scientifically outcomes appear for some practicals.

P.descriptor: Some outcome codes are present but may be incorrect or not clearly matched to what the practical involves. Working Scientifically outcomes are absent or largely missing.

N.descriptor: Outcome codes are missing, incorrect, or do not relate to the practicals listed.

rubric.criterion: Sequence — the order of your practicals

HD.descriptor: Your sequence shows a clear, expert progression — earlier practicals introduce foundations that later ones build on. The rationale for the order is evident from the design itself.

D.descriptor: Your sequence is logical and progressive. A student could work through it without encountering content they are not yet prepared for.

C.descriptor: Your practicals are in a generally sensible order. A small number could be reordered without significant loss, but the overall direction is correct.

P.descriptor: Your practicals are listed but the order does not consistently reflect how student understanding builds. A student could encounter a practical before they have the prior knowledge it requires.

N.descriptor: Your practicals appear in an arbitrary or unclear order with no evidence that the sequence reflects how students learn.

HD.descriptor: A named constructivist approach is clearly identifiable in the overall sequence design. Prior knowledge is specific and accurate for every practical, calibrated to the year group and NSW syllabus.

D.descriptor: A constructivist approach is recognisable in the sequence. Prior knowledge is identified for most practicals and is appropriate to the year group.

C.descriptor: Some evidence of constructivist thinking in the sequencing. Prior knowledge is identified for some practicals, though entries may be general rather than specific to each practical's content.

P.descriptor: Little evidence of a constructivist approach in the design. Prior knowledge entries are listed for few practicals or are too vague to be useful.

N.descriptor: No recognisable constructivist approach. Prior knowledge is absent or irrelevant to the practicals listed.

rubric.criterion: Safety and inclusion for the given cohort

HD.descriptor: Safety information is accurate and specific to each practical. Adjustments are proposed for all four cohort groups and are practical, context-specific, and differentiated from each other — not generic strategies applied uniformly.

D.descriptor: Safety information is accurate across all practicals. Adjustments are proposed for the main cohort groups and are appropriate to the context.

C.descriptor: Safety information is present and mostly accurate. Adjustments address at least two of the four named cohort groups, though they may be generic rather than practical-specific.

P.descriptor: Safety information is present but incomplete or not specific to the practicals chosen. Adjustments attempt to address one cohort group, or are too general to be usable in a real classroom.

N.descriptor: Safety information is absent, incorrect, or references prohibited materials. No meaningful adjustments for the given cohort.

HD.descriptor: Every claim is supported by a precisely cited source. All five elements are addressed with clear reasoning. You explain why each decision serves the given cohort specifically — not just what you decided.

D.descriptor: Most claims are cited. Four of the five elements are addressed with clear reasoning. The connection between your decisions and the given cohort is evident.

C.descriptor: Key claims are cited; some are not. At least three elements are addressed. Your reasoning describes what you did, though not always why it serves the cohort.

P.descriptor: Some citations are present but may be inaccurate or insufficient. Fewer than three elements are addressed, or coverage is too brief to constitute a real justification.

N.descriptor: Sources are absent or unrelated to what you are claiming. The justification does not address the required elements, or is description without reasoning.

HD.descriptor: You identify what was genuinely new and what was already familiar. You name a specific professional learning source and explain exactly why it is relevant to your strand and your teaching context — not why professional learning in general is valuable.

D.descriptor: You name a specific professional learning source and connect it to your strand or teaching context, though the connection may be general. You note both new and prior knowledge.

C.descriptor: You identify a professional learning source, though it may be vague (e.g. 'NESA resources' without naming which). Some reflection on new and prior knowledge is present.

P.descriptor: Your reflection addresses the prompt superficially. A professional learning source is absent or too vague to be meaningful (e.g. 'research more' or 'ask a colleague').

N.descriptor: Not submitted, too brief to assess, or does not address the prompt.

assessmentTasks.hdCallout: HD work in Part A is distinguished by nuance in the prior knowledge column — not a generic entry, but a precise account of what students at this stage of the NSW syllabus should be able to do before this practical. HD work in Part B explains why each decision serves the given cohort specifically, not just what was decided. HD work in Part C names a specific professional learning source and justifies its relevance to this strand and this teaching context.
submissionInstructions: Submit on the template provided via the myLearn assessment portal.
Include an APA 7th edition reference list at the end of your submission.
Excluded from the word count: in-text citations, reference list, and syllabus extracts.
Submissions that exceed the word count by more than 10% may be penalised.
The syllabus used must be the NSW Science Years 7–10 (2023).

assessmentTasks.title: Unit Program
assessmentTasks.rationale: Secondary school science teachers need skills to program a unit of work that can meet the learning needs of a diverse range of students and address syllabus outcomes, skills, and content. Pedagogical decisions will be made on a daily basis, and these will be guided by a learning and teaching program.
assessmentTasks.aim: The aims of this assessment task are for you to demonstrate your understanding of a constructivist pedagogical approach and your ability to interpret syllabus documents to write a unit program for Years 7–10 science. You will also demonstrate your understanding and application of literacy, numeracy, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander histories and perspectives, formative and summative assessment, community engagement, and practical lessons.

parts.title: Unit Program
parts.description: Develop a five-week unit program for the given cohort using a Knowledge and Understanding strand and constructivist pedagogical approach of your choice. Assume four 50-minute lessons per week, two of which are laboratory lessons (20 lessons total).
requirements: Clearly name your constructivist approach — its key features must be recognisable throughout the program
Syllabus outcomes, content, and skills (Working Scientifically)
General Capabilities and Cross Curriculum Priorities
Teaching strategies that engage the diverse cohort
Adjustments for the given cohort
At least two formative assessment opportunities to inform future teaching
At least one practical per week conducted in a laboratory lesson

parts.title: Highlight and Embed
parts.description: Go back through your Part A unit program and apply the following highlights.
requirements: Blue — literacy and numeracy strategies
Yellow — Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, histories and cultures
Green — formative assessment tasks
Highlights must reflect genuine embedding in Part A — retrospective addition will be evident to a marker
At least two formative assessment tasks must be present and highlighted

parts.title: Summative Assessment Task with A–E Marking Rubric
parts.description: Design a summative assessment task that appropriately captures student achievement at the end of your unit.
requirements: The task may be individual or collaborative, theory or practical
Must be completed in class (not take-home)
Must be inclusive for all learners
Must align to syllabus outcomes addressed in Parts A and B
Must include an A–E marking rubric using language from the NESA Science Years 7–10 Performance Band Descriptors

parts.title: Reflection
parts.description: Reflect on what was most and least familiar across unit programming, assessment design, and planning for diverse learners.
requirements: Identify one aspect of your unit program you would evaluate or refine after teaching it, and describe what evidence you would use

Name one external professional or community source — such as a First Nations Australian community representative, a NESA consultant, or a science education professional association — whose input could improve your content knowledge or teaching
Explain briefly how you would access that support

criteria.title: Syllabus outcomes, content, skills, and practicals
criteria.mustShow: Correct NSW syllabus outcome codes mapped to each week's content and activities. Both Knowledge & Understanding and Working Scientifically outcomes present throughout. Practicals appear at least once per week in laboratory lessons and are matched to the content being taught — not added generically.

criteria.title: Teaching sequence, strategies, and adjustments for the given cohort
criteria.mustShow: A named constructivist approach (e.g. 5E, inquiry cycle) that is clearly recognisable in how the five weeks are structured — not just stated in the introduction. Teaching strategies that are specific to the given cohort. A progressive sequence that builds understanding across the unit. Specific, practical adjustments for all four cohort groups: EAL/D (2 students), advanced (2 students), behind (3 students), Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander students (5 students with community connections). General Capabilities and Cross Curriculum Priorities embedded where relevant.

criteria.title: Literacy, numeracy, and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander embedding
criteria.mustShow: Blue highlights on literacy and numeracy strategies that are genuine and specific to the lessons they appear in. Yellow highlights on Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, histories, and cultures that are meaningfully embedded — not tokenistic. Both types of highlighting appear throughout the unit, not clustered in one week.

criteria.title: Inclusion and cohort appropriateness
criteria.mustShow: Evidence that you have thought about how each group in the given cohort (EAL/D, advanced, behind, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander) can access the learning in your unit. Specific barriers identified and addressed — not generic statements about inclusion.

criteria.title: Formative assessment tasks
criteria.mustShow: At least two formative assessment tasks highlighted in green. Each task is clearly connected to a specific learning goal and is designed to generate evidence you could use to adjust your teaching for the given cohort — not simply any activity that checks understanding.

criteria.title: Task design and syllabus alignment
criteria.mustShow: A clearly named task with the correct year group, a logical issue date and submission date, time allowed in class, and a level of difficulty appropriate for the cohort. The task is explicitly linked to the syllabus outcomes addressed in Parts A and B. Student-facing instructions are unambiguous — a student should be able to read them and immediately understand what is required.

criteria.title: Internal alignment and appropriateness
criteria.mustShow: A task that is clearly drawn from what you taught in your unit program in Parts A and B. The task design gives every member of the given cohort a genuine opportunity to demonstrate their learning, with access considerations built in rather than added as an afterthought.

criteria.title: Marking rubric quality
criteria.mustShow: An A–E marking rubric that uses language from the NESA Science Years 7–10 Performance Band Descriptors at each grade level. Descriptors that are specific enough that a marker could use them to arrive at a consistent grade. A student reading the rubric should be able to identify exactly what they need to do to move from one grade to the next.

criteria.title: Professional reflection and learning
criteria.mustShow: Genuine reflection on what was most and least familiar across unit programming, assessment design, and planning for diverse learners. Three specific things: (1) one specific aspect of your unit program you would evaluate or refine after teaching it, with a clear description of what evidence you would use; (2) one named, specific external professional or community source whose input could improve your content knowledge or teaching of this unit; (3) a brief explanation of how you would actually access that support.

rubric.criterion: Syllabus outcomes, content, skills, and practicals

HD.descriptor: Outcome codes are correct throughout all five weeks. Every lesson and practical is matched to specific content statements. Working Scientifically outcomes, General Capabilities, and Cross Curriculum Priorities are included where genuinely relevant. Practicals are expertly integrated and purposefully matched to what is being taught.

D.descriptor: Outcome codes are correct. Content, skills, and practicals are well-matched throughout. Working Scientifically outcomes are included for most weeks. Practicals are well-integrated.

C.descriptor: Outcome codes are mostly correct with minor errors. Practicals are present and appropriate. Working Scientifically outcomes appear for some weeks. Sound content knowledge overall.

P.descriptor: Some outcome codes are present but may be incorrect or mismatched to lesson content. Practicals are present but may not align to the content being taught. Adequate content knowledge with some gaps.

N.descriptor: Syllabus elements are inaccurate or largely missing. Practicals are absent, inadequate, or poorly matched. Content knowledge is not adequately demonstrated.

rubric.criterion: Teaching sequence, strategies, and adjustments for the given cohort

HD.descriptor: Constructivist approach is named and its key features are sustained and clearly recognisable across all five weeks. Teaching strategies are innovative and specifically responsive to the given cohort. The sequence builds conceptual understanding with expert progression. Adjustments are nuanced, specific, and differentiated for all four cohort groups. GCs and CCPs are purposefully embedded.

D.descriptor: Constructivist approach is named and well-applied throughout. Teaching strategies are effective and cohort-responsive. The sequence builds understanding well. Accurate adjustments for the main cohort groups. GCs and CCPs are embedded.

C.descriptor: Constructivist approach is named and evident. Teaching strategies are appropriate. The sequence is mostly progressive. Sound adjustments for most cohort groups. GCs and CCPs are present.

P.descriptor: Constructivist approach is referenced but only partially evident in the sequence. Some strategies are appropriate. Progression is adequate. Limited adjustments; GCs and CCPs are partially addressed.

N.descriptor: Constructivist approach is not named or not evident in the sequence design. Strategies are limited or inappropriate for the cohort. The sequence does not build understanding. Adjustments are absent or inadequate.

rubric.criterion: Literacy, numeracy, and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander embedding

HD.descriptor: An exceptional range of literacy and numeracy strategies and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives genuinely and effectively embedded throughout. All elements highlighted.

D.descriptor: A strong range effectively embedded throughout the unit. All elements highlighted.

C.descriptor: Sound embedding with appropriate strategies and perspectives across the unit. All elements are highlighted.

P.descriptor: Limited strategies embedded. Most elements are highlighted but coverage is thin.

N.descriptor: Strategies are absent or not highlighted.

HD.descriptor: Barriers to participation are expertly identified and mitigated for all cohort groups. The unit provides genuinely equitable access and challenge for every learner.

D.descriptor: Barriers are identified and mitigated. The unit provides clear access for all diverse learners.

C.descriptor: Some barriers are identified. The unit is mostly accessible for diverse learners.

P.descriptor: Limited attention to barriers. Some adjustments are present but coverage is thin.

N.descriptor: Barriers are not identified. The unit does not address the diverse learner needs of the given cohort.

HD.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted. Each is purposefully designed with an explicit connection to learning goals and a clear link to how the evidence would inform subsequent teaching for the given cohort.

D.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted. Well-chosen; the connection to future teaching is clearly evident.

C.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted. Appropriate; some indication of how they inform teaching.

P.descriptor: Two or more tasks present and highlighted, but they are generic or loosely connected to learning goals.

N.descriptor: Fewer than two tasks present or highlighted, or the tasks identified would not generate meaningful evidence to inform teaching.

HD.descriptor: Task details are accurate and clearly appropriate for the cohort. Alignment to syllabus outcomes from the unit is exceptional. Instructions are unambiguous, precise, and inclusive — a student could begin working immediately.

D.descriptor: Task details are accurate and appropriate. Alignment to syllabus outcomes is strong. Instructions are clear and concise.

C.descriptor: Task details are accurate. Alignment to syllabus outcomes is sound. Instructions are mostly clear.

P.descriptor: Task details are mostly accurate. Alignment to some outcomes is adequate. Instructions are present but have room for improvement.

N.descriptor: Errors in task details. Alignment is inadequate. Instructions are ambiguous or absent.

HD.descriptor: The task aligns closely and purposefully to the unit program in Parts A and B. All cohort members have a genuine, equitable opportunity to demonstrate achievement. Inclusion is central to the task design.

D.descriptor: The task aligns well to Parts A and B. Cohort members have a clear opportunity to demonstrate achievement.

C.descriptor: The task aligns to Parts A and B. Most cohort members can demonstrate achievement, though access for some learners may be limited.

P.descriptor: The task aligns adequately to Parts A and B but may not fully reflect the outcomes taught, or may not provide equitable access for all cohort members.

N.descriptor: The task does not align to the unit program in Parts A and B, and/or does not provide an adequate opportunity for the given cohort to demonstrate their learning.

HD.descriptor: The rubric strongly supports consistent marking and clear student understanding. NESA Performance Band Descriptor language is used very effectively across all five bands. The distinction between adjacent bands is precise and observable.

D.descriptor: The rubric supports consistent marking and student understanding. NESA Performance Band Descriptor language is used well across the five bands.

C.descriptor: The rubric provides some support for consistent marking. NESA Performance Band Descriptor language is used soundly.

P.descriptor: The rubric is present but may be poorly constructed. NESA Performance Band Descriptor language is attempted.

N.descriptor: The rubric is absent, very poorly constructed, or NESA Performance Band Descriptor language has not been used.

HD.descriptor: Exceptional reflection: a specific evaluation aspect is identified with precise evidence articulated. The external source is well-chosen, named specifically, and convincingly justified in relation to your content knowledge or teaching of this unit. Access is explained concretely.

D.descriptor: Critical reflection: an evaluation aspect is identified with evidence noted. An external source is named and justified; the connection to your teaching of this unit is evident.

C.descriptor: Some critical reflection: an evaluation aspect is noted. An external source is identified with some justification, though it may be general rather than specific to your unit or strand.

P.descriptor: Surface reflection: an evaluation aspect or external source is absent, or both are too vague to assess — for example, 'I would reflect on my teaching' or 'I would consult colleagues'.

N.descriptor: Not submitted, too brief to assess, or does not address the prompt.

assessmentTasks.hdCallout: HD work in Part A sustains the named constructivist approach across all five weeks — not just stated in the introduction. HD work in Part B shows highlights that are genuinely woven into the teaching strategies, not applied retrospectively. HD work in Part C produces rubric descriptors specific enough that two markers could independently arrive at the same grade. HD work in Part D names a specific external professional or community source and explains concretely how it would be accessed — not a category or a general statement about professional learning.
submissionInstructions: Submit on the template provided via the myLearn assessment portal.
Include an APA 7th edition reference list at the end of your submission.

Excluded from the word count: in-text citations, reference list, syllabus extracts, and the unit program overview section (year group, strand, pedagogical approach, and rationale — typically before the weekly tables).
Do NOT write 20 lesson plans. This is a high-level unit program.
Submissions that exceed the word count by more than 10% may be penalised.
The syllabus used must be the NSW Science Years 7–10 (2023).

supportCallout: Anything going on that may impact your success in this unit? Don't hesitate to reach out to your lecturer sooner rather than later. We're here to support you.

announcementBody.intro: Welcome to EDSE362. This unit develops your capacity to design curriculum, choose pedagogy, and teach inclusively across senior science — building the professional judgement you will draw on every time you plan for a real and diverse classroom. Over the trimester you will move from examining what the syllabus asks of you to making and defending your own decisions about how to teach it well for every student.
announcementBody.focus: Before Topic 1 begins next week, take time to explore how the unit fits together. Open the Unit Outline and read the assessment overview, find the Learning Materials and Assessment Portal in the navigation block, and note the day and time of your weekly live session. Look ahead to the two assessment tasks now — AT1 is due 26 July and AT2 on 6 September, and both reward work that begins early rather than late in the trimester.

1.title: The K–10 Continuum and Science Education

recordings.label: Topic 1 – The K–10 Continuum and Science Education
Topic 2 – The Nature of Science and Science Teaching

1.liveSessionFocus: Session 1 covers Topics 1 and 2 together. We will examine the K–10 continuum and the Nature of Science as connected foundations for planning intellectually honest and engaging senior science lessons.
liveSessionTasks: Read this scenario: a Year 8 student says 'just tell me the answer — why do we have to do the experiment?' Jot down how you would respond in the moment.

Now open the Science 7–10 Syllabus (2023) and find one Working Scientifically skill and one focus-area outcome that you could point to in support of your response. The nature of science lives in these places rather than in a strand of its own.
Be ready to discuss: how does the way the syllabus embeds the nature of science change what you'd actually say to that student?

sections.heading: Key Outcomes
items: The inaugural live session introduced the unit's focus on translating science education foundations into classroom practice, covering the K–10 curriculum continuum and the nature of science.
Participants discussed the primary-to-secondary transition, formative assessment, practical investigations, and the relevance of science in a post-truth world.
Assessment Task 1 — designing a sequence of practical science lessons using a constructivist approach — was introduced at a high level, with a detailed unpacking video to follow.

sections.heading: Decisions Made
items: AT1 requires a sequenced series of practical lessons targeting specific working scientifically outcomes within a chosen module — not all skills at once.
Practicals should be described in the submission; appendices only needed for bespoke or unusual experiments not findable online.
Formative assessment should be woven throughout the entire learning cycle, not only used diagnostically at the start of Year 7.
Closed investigations with a narrow skill focus are appropriate for building working scientifically skills progressively; open inquiry has a place but requires sufficient background knowledge first.

sections.heading: Key Discussion Themes
items: Primary–secondary transition: Limited science time in K–6, variable teacher science expertise, and inconsistent practical experience create significant knowledge gaps by Year 7.

Intended vs. enacted curriculum: The syllabus mandates ~60% investigations, but student reports suggest far fewer practicals actually occur — attributed to time constraints, risk management, and teacher workload.
Nature of science: Framed as a process (questioning, collecting data, analysing, communicating) rather than a collection of facts; working scientifically outcomes seen as its practical embodiment.

Science relevance: Students disengaging when they cannot connect science to their lives; nurturing curiosity and inquiry skills positioned as an antidote to passive, algorithm-driven knowledge consumption.
Safety vs. practicality tension: Schools have low risk appetite; collaborative planning with lab techs and department heads is key to maximising practical frequency.

sections.heading: Open Questions
items: To what extent are cross-curriculum priorities (e.g., Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander histories, critical and creative thinking) genuinely embedded vs. less visible in the 2023 NSW syllabus compared to prior versions?
At what stage is it realistic for Year 7 students to design their own experiments? Consensus: possible by end of Year 7 with sufficient scaffolding for varying ability levels.

sections.heading: Pending Confirmation
items: Detailed Assessment Task 1 unpacking video to be released by the following week.
Word limit for Assessment Task 1 is tight; lecturer will not strictly police minor overruns.

sections.heading: Action Items
items: All students: Review the constructive alignment map in unit resources; note how learning outcomes align to AITSL graduate teaching standards.
All students: Complete readings for Topic 3 — Selecting and Critically Evaluating Resources ahead of next session.
Stephen: Produce and publish Assessment Task 1 unpacking video by next week.
Next session: Topic 3 — Selecting and critically evaluating resources.

topics.title: The K–10 Continuum and Science Education

announcementBody.intro: Welcome to EDSE362. This week we begin by examining the full K–10 science continuum — the foundation of prior knowledge and experience your secondary students bring with them into Stage 4 & 5.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 1 examines how the NSW Science K–6 Science and Technology syllabus connects to the Stage 4 & 5 Science syllabuses. You will trace the progression of key concepts, skills, and Working Scientifically strands from primary through to early secondary science, and consider how understanding this continuum helps you plan more responsive and connected Stage 6 lessons.

topics.orientationNote: Topic 1 provides the syllabus literacy you need for both assessment tasks. In AT1 Part A, every row of your practical table requires you to identify specific NSW Science Years 7–10 (2023) outcome codes and content statements, and to articulate the prior knowledge students bring to each practical — both of which depend on your ability to read the continuum, not just the strand you have chosen. Quality work in AT1 Part A is distinguished by nuance in the prior knowledge column: not 'students know about forces' but a precise account of what Stage 4 students should be able to do before encountering Stage 5 content. Use this week to build that precision.
forumPrompts: Open the NSW Science Years 7–10 (2023) syllabus and locate a Knowledge and Understanding strand you are considering for AT1. Choose one content point from that strand and trace its conceptual lineage back through the K–10 continuum — what would a student in Stage 3 have encountered that underpins it? What might they have misunderstood or only partially developed? Post the content point, your trace, and one specific prior knowledge assumption you would build into your AT1 Part A table for that practical.

The given cohort for AT1 includes students working at different points on the achievement spectrum — two advanced, three behind, two EAL/D. For one practical you are considering for AT1 Part A, identify what 'behind' might specifically look like for that practical: not a general deficit, but a particular gap in prior conceptual understanding or practical skill that would affect how that student engages with the investigation. What does this tell you about where that practical should sit in your sequence?
topics.workedExample: The following examples show the difference between a strong and a partial prior knowledge entry in the AT1 Part A table. Both are for a Stage 4 practical investigating how changes to one component of an ecosystem affect a living system.Strong (Living systems – Stage 4):“Students should already understand from Stage 3 that living things depend on each other and on their environment for survival. They should be able to identify producers and consumers in simple food chains and recognise that changes in environmental conditions can affect living things. However, students commonly think ecosystems are stable and self-correcting rather than dynamic systems that change over time. This practical assumes students can make systematic observations, organise simple data tables and identify patterns from evidence using Working Scientifically skills developed in primary science and early Stage 4.”Partial (Living systems – Stage 4):“Students know about ecosystems and have done science experiments before.”The partial entry identifies prior learning only in general terms. It does not identify the specific conceptual understandings students should bring to the investigation, the misconceptions likely to affect learning, or the practical skills required for success.
topics.synthesisTemplate: Thanks everyone for your Topic 1 forum contributions. Some careful syllabus reading coming through — particularly in the prior knowledge traces. A pattern to note: [OBSERVATION — e.g. many entries named a Stage 4 concept correctly but didn't specify the outcome code or identify where the conceptual gap tends to sit for students at this transition]. One thing to take into the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. the difference between 'students know about X' and 'students can do Y with X' — the prior knowledge column is asking for the second one]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

topics.title: The Nature of Science and Science Teaching

announcementBody.intro: This week we turn to the epistemological foundations of science itself — what science is, how it works, and what it means to teach it honestly and rigorously in a senior secondary context.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 2 explores the Nature of Science (NOS) as a professional and pedagogical framework. You will examine the values, practices, and commitments that distinguish science as a way of knowing, and consider how a clear understanding of NOS shapes the way you select content, design investigations, and communicate the purpose of science to students.

topics.orientationNote: Topic 2 directly supports the justification you will write in AT1 Part B and the pedagogical framing of AT2 Part A. In Part B, you are asked to justify your practical sequence with reference to pedagogical principles — and the Working Scientifically strand is one of the most credible justificatory frameworks available, because it is built into the syllabus. A strong Part B does not cite inquiry-based learning in the abstract; it identifies specific Working Scientifically outcomes that your practicals address and explains why the sequence develops scientific thinking progressively. This week, practise mapping NOS concepts to specific content and skills — that habit of mind is exactly what distinguishes a DI justification from a CR one.
forumPrompts: The NSW Science Years 7–10 (2023) syllabus embeds Working Scientifically outcomes across all strands. Choose one practical you are considering for AT1 Part A. Identify which Working Scientifically outcomes it addresses — be specific about the outcome code and the aspect of scientific inquiry the practical develops (e.g. designing investigations, processing data, communicating). Then identify one aspect of the Nature of Science this practical could make explicit to students if you chose to foreground it. Post your practical title, the WS outcomes, and your NOS connection.

A Year 9 student asks: 'Why do we have to keep doing experiments when the textbook already has the answers?' Write the response you would give — not a dismissive one, and not a lecture on epistemology, but a genuine answer grounded in your understanding of what science is and why the process matters. Your response should be something you could actually say to a 15-year-old. Post it, then identify which aspect of the Nature of Science your answer implicitly relies on.
topics.workedExample: The following examples show the difference between a strong and a partial Working Scientifically outcome identification in AT1 Part A. Both are for a Stage 5 practical investigating how changing the frequency of a wave affects its behaviour.Strong (Waves and motion – Stage 5):“Working Scientifically outcomes: students develop a prediction about how changing frequency may affect wave behaviour before collecting evidence; they organise and analyse observations using tables and graphs; and they communicate evidence-based conclusions using appropriate scientific terminology. Knowledge and Understanding content: properties and behaviour of waves. Nature of Science connection: students compare their predictions with the evidence collected and refine their explanations, demonstrating how scientific knowledge develops through testing ideas against evidence.”Partial (Waves and motion – Stage 5):“Students follow the method, collect results and learn about waves.”The partial entry describes activities rather than outcomes. It does not identify the scientific thinking being developed or explain how the investigation contributes to students’ understanding of how science works.
topics.synthesisTemplate: Thanks for your Topic 2 posts this week. The NOS-to-WS mapping exercise surfaced some discipline-specific patterns worth discussing. A common thread: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most posts identified a WS outcome accurately but found it harder to name a specific NOS concept the practical makes explicit — that gap is worth sitting with]. One thing to bring to the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. the difference between a practical that uses scientific processes and one that makes the nature of those processes visible to students]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

2.title: Selecting and Critically Evaluating Resources

announcementBody.intro: This week we develop your skills as a critical consumer and selector of teaching resources — a practical and ongoing professional responsibility that shapes the quality of every lesson you teach.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 3 examines how to evaluate resources systematically and critically across different formats: textbooks, digital tools, laboratory kits, online platforms, and teacher-created materials. You will develop a personal evaluation framework grounded in syllabus alignment, scientific accuracy, and pedagogical appropriateness, and begin building a curated resource library for your teaching area.

2.liveSessionFocus: Session 3 focuses on resource evaluation frameworks and strategies for building a coherent, high-quality teaching toolkit across the Stage 6 science syllabuses.
liveSessionTasks: Select one textbook or digital resource used in your primary teaching area and evaluate it against your own criteria for quality — consider syllabus alignment, scientific accuracy, and how it supports student thinking.
Identify one specific strength and one clear weakness in the resource, and consider what you would do to address the weakness in practice.
Come prepared to share your evaluation briefly — we will compare criteria and approaches across different teaching areas in the session.
2.orientationNote: Topic 3 is directly assessed in AT1 Part B, which requires you to cite relevant literature and sources to justify your practical sequence. The quality of your justification depends on whether you can locate, evaluate, and deploy sources with precision — not just include them. A Part B that earns HD cites sources that are genuinely relevant to the specific pedagogical decisions made in Part A: the choice of constructivist approach, the safety framework used, the adjustments designed for the cohort. A Part B that earns CR cites sources but does not connect them clearly to specific decisions. Use this week to build your evaluation criteria before you need them under time pressure.
forumPrompts: Locate one resource — textbook chapter, journal article, curriculum document, or online platform — that you could cite in AT1 Part B to justify either (a) your choice of constructivist pedagogical approach, or (b) a specific safety decision for one of your practicals. Evaluate it against these three criteria: syllabus alignment (does it engage with the NSW Science Years 7–10 context?), scientific accuracy (is the content reliable?), and pedagogical credibility (is the justification for the approach evidence-based?). Post the reference, your three-criterion evaluation, and a sentence showing how you would use it in Part B.

You are reviewing a Year 8 Chemistry practical resource that instructs students to heat copper sulfate solution over a Bunsen burner without specifying eye protection requirements. Identify: (a) the specific safety gap; (b) the authoritative source you would consult to determine the correct requirement (e.g. CLEAPSS, RACI, your school's safety documents); (c) how you would record this in the safety column of your AT1 Part A table. What does this process tell you about the difference between a resource that is pedagogically useful and one that is safe to use?
2.workedExample: The following examples show the difference between a strong and a partial citation in AT1 Part B. Both are justifying the placement of a Forces investigation early in a Stage 4 sequence.Strong (Forces – Stage 4):“The investigation examining balanced and unbalanced forces is positioned early in the sequence because students already possess everyday experiences of pushing, pulling and motion from primary science. Beginning with observable phenomena allows students to build scientific explanations from familiar experiences before introducing more abstract representations. The sequence is informed by Bybee’s (2014) 5E model, particularly the Explore phase, where students test their predictions and confront misconceptions such as ‘constant motion requires a constant force’. Visual prompts, annotated diagrams and structured discussion scaffolds are included to support EAL/D learners while maintaining the same conceptual expectations.”Partial (Forces – Stage 4):“The practicals are sequenced using constructivist learning principles. Students learn best when they build on prior knowledge. Forces is taught early because it is an important topic.”The partial response cites a general principle but does not connect the source to a specific teaching decision, identify the relevant prior knowledge, or explain why the investigation sits where it does in the sequence.
2.synthesisTemplate: Thanks for your Topic 3 forum posts this week. The resource evaluation activity is producing some strong critical thinking. A pattern worth noting: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most evaluations identified scientific accuracy and syllabus alignment well, but the pedagogical credibility criterion was often treated as 'does it look teachable' rather than 'is there evidence this approach supports learning']. One thing to bring to the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. the distinction between a resource that is convenient and one that is defensible — Part B of AT1 asks you to defend your decisions with sources, not just describe them]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

3.title: Introduction to Teaching Differently

announcementBody.intro: This week we turn our attention to one of the most powerful tools in any teacher's repertoire — questioning. Not just asking questions, but designing them deliberately to do different cognitive work. We look at why open questions reveal thinking while closed questions shut it down, how Bloom's Taxonomy and the Q-Matrix can sharpen your questions across different levels of cognitive demand, and what psychological safety has to do with whether students answer at all. The central idea this week, from Eddie Woo: the person doing the talking is the person doing the learning.

3.liveSessionFocus: Designing questions that shift cognitive work to students — from closed recall to open inquiry
liveSessionTasks: Work through the Topic 4 readings and lecture before the live session — pay particular attention to the Koufetta-Menicou & Scaife (2000) classification table of nine question types
Watch the Eddie Woo video and note one phrase or moment that changes how you think about classroom talk

Identify which question types from the Koufetta-Menicou & Scaife classification you currently use most in your teaching or placements, and which you rarely use — bring your reflection to the live session
Complete at least one of the three forum activities before or during the live session
3.orientationNote: Topic 4 sits at the midpoint of the unit and marks a shift — from what you teach to how you teach it. The questioning techniques you develop here will show up in every lesson you ever run. They are directly relevant to Assessment Task 1: the practical activities you design in Part B and the Depth Study in Part C both require deliberate questioning moments — at the toolbox safety briefing, during investigation setup, and in the closing reflection. As you work through the materials, keep asking: what would I actually say to a class of Year 9 students at this moment?
forumPrompts: Choose any practical for any Year group from any source. Identify the key questioning moments in a lesson built around it: (a) What would you ask to open the lesson and activate prior knowledge? (b) What questions would you use in the toolbox safety briefing? (c) What questions would engage students during the investigation itself? (d) What reflective question would close the lesson? Record 3–5 questions with a brief reason for each, then post to the forum.

Using the Q-Matrix or Bloom's Taxonomy as a guide, design a sequence of 4 questions of increasing cognitive demand about a topic from your science discipline. Start at Knowledge/Remembering and work toward at least Analysis or Evaluation level. Post your sequence and explain the progression — why does the order matter?

Locate an image online that could be used as an engagement hook in a Year 7–10 science classroom. Record the URL. Devise 3 questions you would use with students: one to elicit current understanding, one to surface possible misconceptions, one to push higher-order thinking. Post the image URL and your three questions with a brief explanation of each.
3.synthesisTemplate: Thanks everyone for your Topic 4 forum contributions this week. Some strong questioning sequences coming through. A few patterns worth noting across the cohort: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most sequences started well at recall level but the jump to analysis was sometimes abrupt — consider what bridging question sits in between]. One thing to take into the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. the distinction between an open question and a genuinely divergent question — open doesn't automatically mean high cognitive demand]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

4.title: The Inclusive Science Classroom

announcementBody.intro: Building on Topic 4, this week we move from the theory of differentiation into the practical challenge of creating genuinely inclusive senior science classrooms — environments where every student can access, engage with, and be challenged by the curriculum.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 5 examines inclusive teaching in the senior science context, with a focus on students with disability, students from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds, and students at both ends of the achievement spectrum. You will explore specific adjustments, design principles, and communication strategies, and consider how to maintain academic rigour while broadening access.

4.liveSessionFocus: Session 5 focuses on the practical design of inclusive learning experiences for senior science, including how to adapt tasks, resources, and assessment approaches without reducing the challenge of Stage 6 content.
liveSessionTasks: Identify one adjustment or scaffolding strategy from the topic materials that you could apply immediately to a senior science lesson you are planning or have taught.
Consider how Stage 6 syllabus requirements interact with NESA access arrangements — where do they align well, and where do tensions arise?
Come prepared to discuss a specific scenario: a student with a particular learning need in your senior science class. What would an inclusive response look like?
4.orientationNote: Topic 5 is directly assessed across AT1 Part A and AT2 Parts A and B — and it is where the majority of students lose marks. The given cohort is specified precisely: 2 EAL/D, 2 advanced, 3 behind, 5 Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander students with strong local community connections. In AT1 Part A, each practical requires specific adjustments for this cohort — not generic strategies, but modifications that respond to the actual demands of that practical. In AT2 Part B, the yellow highlight requires genuine embedding of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, not retrospective addition. This week, practise designing adjustments that preserve the scientific rigour of the task while genuinely broadening access.
forumPrompts: For one practical you are designing for AT1 Part A, identify adjustments for each of the four cohort groups: EAL/D students (2), advanced students (2), students working behind (3), and Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander students (5). For each group, the adjustment must be specific to that practical — not a generic strategy that would apply to any science lesson. Post your practical title and your four adjustments, then identify which adjustment you found hardest to design and why.

The AT2 Part B yellow highlight requires embedding Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, histories, and cultures into your unit program — not as an add-on activity, but woven into the learning sequence itself. Choose any science strand from the NSW Years 7–10 syllabus and identify one specific content point where a genuine connection to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander knowledge could be embedded in a way that strengthens the science learning, not just acknowledges it exists. Describe what that would look like in a lesson, and identify a source — ideally a community-connected or AIATSIS resource — that would inform your approach. What is the difference between embedding and tokenism in this context?
4.workedExample: The following examples show the difference between strong and partial cohort adjustments in AT1 Part A. Both are for a Stage 4 practical investigating cell structure using microscopy.Strong (Cells and classification – Stage 4):EAL/D adjustment – “Provide a visual glossary of microscope parts and cell structures, with labelled diagrams accompanying all written instructions. Procedural steps are presented as numbered images and short sentences.”Advanced adjustment – “Students compare specialised cell types and explain how structural differences support different biological functions, drawing on additional scientific sources.”Behind adjustment – “Students use partially labelled diagrams and focus initially on identifying a small number of key structures before progressing to more detailed observations.”Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adjustment – “Students investigate the microscopic structure of a locally significant plant species selected with appropriate community guidance, examining both biological characteristics and cultural significance.”Partial:EAL/D – “Provide a glossary.”Advanced – “Give extension questions.”Behind – “Simplify the activity.”Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander – “Include Indigenous perspectives.”The partial adjustments are generic and could apply to almost any lesson. They demonstrate awareness that differentiation is required but do not show how barriers to participation are being addressed within the context of this specific practical investigation.
4.synthesisTemplate: Thanks for your Topic 5 contributions this week. The cohort adjustment activity is the most directly assessed skill in the unit — it shows up in AT1 Part A and across AT2 Parts A and B. A pattern in the posts: [OBSERVATION — e.g. adjustments for EAL/D students tended to be strong and specific, but the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adjustments were often generic — 'connect to Country' without identifying a specific connection to the practical or a community source]. One thing to bring to the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. the difference between an adjustment that reduces barriers to participation and one that reduces the challenge — the rubric rewards the first; the second is a different problem]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

5.title: Planning and Evaluating a Unit of Work — Part 1: The Planning Process

announcementBody.intro: This week we bring together your curriculum knowledge, pedagogical understanding, and resource evaluation skills to focus on the discipline of unit planning — one of the most important and complex professional tasks you will undertake as a science teacher.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 6 examines how to design, sequence, and evaluate a coherent unit of work for Stage 6 science. You will work through the key decisions involved in unit planning: establishing outcomes, selecting and sequencing content, designing assessments for and of learning, choosing resources, and building in opportunities for student reflection and feedback.

5.liveSessionFocus: Session 6 covers Topics 5 and 6 together. We will examine inclusive practice and unit planning as interconnected professional responsibilities, and work through the decisions that shape a coherent, accessible, and outcomes-aligned Stage 6 unit.
liveSessionTasks: Select a topic or module from your Stage 6 syllabus and sketch a rough unit outline — identify the key outcomes, main content sequence, and at least one formative and one summative assessment task.
Evaluate your outline against the unit planning criteria in the topic materials: Is it coherent? Is it outcomes-focused? Does it build in opportunities for feedback?
Come prepared to share your outline and receive peer feedback in the session — bring a willingness to revise.
5.orientationNote: Topic 6A is the direct preparation week for Parts A and B of AT2. These sections require you to design a coherent five-week unit program that aligns syllabus outcomes, Working Scientifically skills, practical investigations, differentiation, literacy, numeracy, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives, and formative assessment. The strongest submissions use backwards design principles: beginning with outcomes and constructing a logical sequence of learning rather than assembling disconnected activities. This week focuses on the professional craft of programming and provides the foundation upon which the remainder of AT2 is built.
forumPrompts: A colleague shows you a unit program for Stage 4 Earth and Space Science. The program has five weeks of content, a test at the end, and a note that says 'literacy: students read from textbook each lesson.' Identify three specific improvements you would make to strengthen the program's alignment with AT2 requirements — name the change, the AT2 part it addresses, and the principle behind it.
5.workedExample: An improvement would be to strengthen the literacy component. Instead of simply reading from the textbook each lesson, students could analyse scientific texts, interpret diagrams, construct evidence-based explanations, and explicitly learn Earth and Space Science vocabulary. This addresses AT2 Part A (teaching and learning sequence design) because literacy strategies are intentionally embedded within learning activities rather than treated as an add-on. The principle behind this change is that literacy development occurs through meaningful engagement with disciplinary knowledge and practices.
5.synthesisTemplate: Thanks for your Topic 6 posts — particularly the summative assessment design activity, which is direct preparation for AT2 Part C. Patterns worth noting: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most task designs were clearly connected to content, but the A-band descriptors in the rubrics tended to use adjectives ('thorough', 'detailed') rather than observable content-specific descriptions — NESA Performance Band Descriptor language asks for the second]. One thing to take into the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. ask yourself: could two markers independently arrive at the same grade using your rubric? If not, it needs more specificity]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

6.title: Planning and Evaluating a Unit of Work — Part 2: The Role of Assessment and Evaluation

announcementBody.intro: This week we examine one of the most powerful — and most frequently misused — pedagogical tools available to science teachers: the analogy. Used well, analogies make abstract science concepts concrete and accessible. Used poorly, they embed misconceptions that are hard to shift.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 7 focuses on analogies as tools for conceptual change in science learning. You will examine the theory behind why analogies work, explore frameworks for selecting and presenting analogies effectively (including the Teaching-With-Analogies model), and critically evaluate common analogies used in senior science for their accuracy, accessibility, and potential to mislead.

6.liveSessionFocus: Session 7 examines analogies as pedagogical tools in depth — working through examples from across the senior science syllabuses and practising the skill of evaluating an analogy before using it with students.
liveSessionTasks: Identify one analogy commonly used in your Stage 6 teaching area — write down what concept it is meant to explain, what it maps correctly, and where the mapping breaks down.
Consider how you would introduce this analogy to a class while explicitly addressing its limitations, so students build accurate conceptual understanding rather than a flawed mental model.
Come prepared to present your analogy briefly and receive feedback from peers on how they experience it as learners.
6.orientationNote: Topic 6B is the direct preparation week for Parts C and D of AT2. Part C requires you to design a summative assessment task and A–E marking rubric that align directly with the learning experiences and outcomes developed in your unit program. Part D requires you to evaluate aspects of your teaching program and identify professional sources of support that could improve future practice. Use this week’s materials to think about assessment as evidence for learning, not simply grading, and about evaluation as an ongoing process of refining teaching to improve student outcomes.
forumPrompts: Design a single summative assessment task for a five-week Stage 4 or 5 science unit of your choice. The task should be completable in one lesson or as a short take-home piece, and should assess at least two syllabus outcomes. Post: the task prompt (as you would give it to students), the outcomes it assesses, and one marking criterion written in A–E NESA Performance Band Descriptor language. For your A-band criterion, what does 'extensive knowledge and understanding' actually look like for this task — in concrete, observable terms?
What is the difference between a program that includes formative assessment and one that uses it to inform future teaching?
6.workedExample: The following examples show the difference between a strong and a partial A–E criterion for a Stage 5 Chemistry assessment task asking students to explain a chemical reaction using particle theory.Strong (Chemistry — Stage 5, particle theory task): 'A student working at the A standard provides a scientifically accurate and detailed explanation of the reaction at the particle level, correctly identifying the role of collision frequency, activation energy, and particle orientation. The explanation is coherent and uses appropriate chemical terminology throughout. Diagrams, where included, accurately represent particle arrangement and movement. The student draws explicit connections between the particle-level explanation and observable macroscopic properties (e.g. colour change, gas production, temperature change).'Partial (Chemistry — Stage 5, particle theory task): 'A: Excellent explanation of particle theory. Very detailed and uses correct terminology.' The partial criterion is evaluative but not descriptive — it tells a student they did well without explaining what 'excellent' or 'very detailed' means in terms of content. A student reading this cannot self-assess, and a second marker cannot apply it consistently. NESA Performance Band Descriptor language requires observable, content-specific descriptions of what the student can do — not adjectives about quality.
6.synthesisTemplate: Thanks for your Topic 7 posts this week. The analogy evaluation exercise is producing some careful thinking. A common pattern: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most posts identified what the analogy maps correctly and where it breaks down, but the script for addressing limitations with students was often too abstract — 'I would tell them where it doesn't work' rather than a specific two-sentence approach]. One thing to bring to the live session: [FOCUS — e.g. the moment when a student over-extends an analogy into territory where it breaks down — how do you catch and correct that in real time without undermining the analogy's usefulness?]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

7.title: Teaching and Learning Science Using Analogies

announcementBody.intro: In our final teaching week, we return to a foundational professional responsibility: the ongoing development of your own science content knowledge. Strong content knowledge is not just a starting point — it is something you actively build and deepen throughout your teaching career.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 8 focuses on content knowledge development as a professional discipline. You will conduct a systematic audit of your content knowledge against your Stage 6 syllabus, identify specific gaps and priorities, and build a realistic plan for ongoing learning — including professional associations, subject-matter resources, and peer networks that support science content development.

7.liveSessionFocus: Session 8 covers Topics 7 and 8 together. We will examine analogies and content knowledge development as complementary professional skills, and conclude the teaching program by looking ahead to the professional learning habits that will sustain your practice.
liveSessionTasks: Complete a content knowledge audit for your primary Stage 6 teaching area: rate your confidence across the major content sections and identify your two or three priority gaps.
For each priority gap, identify at least one concrete resource or action you will take to address it — a specific book, online course, professional association, or peer with relevant expertise.
Come prepared to briefly share your audit findings and your plan — and to give and receive constructive feedback with peers.
7.orientationNote: Topic 7 builds directly on the pedagogical justification skills you need for AT1 Part B and the teaching strategy decisions in AT2 Part A. In AT1 Part B, you are asked to justify your choice of pedagogical approach — and the Teaching-With-Analogies model is one framework that can legitimately ground that justification, provided you use it precisely. In AT2 Part A, you are designing five weeks of lessons with named teaching strategies; analogies and models are among the most powerful conceptual-change strategies available in science, but only if their limitations are explicitly addressed. This week, practise the critical evaluation of an analogy before you use it — a habit that will distinguish your AT2 unit program from one that simply lists activities.
forumPrompts: Identify one analogy commonly used in a 7–10 science classroom in your discipline (e.g. 'the atom is like a solar system', 'the cell membrane is like a security guard', 'electric current is like water in a pipe'). For each of these three criteria, evaluate your chosen analogy: (a) What does the analogy map correctly — where is the structural correspondence genuinely accurate? (b) Where does the mapping break down — what misconception could it embed if not explicitly addressed? (c) How would you introduce the analogy with its limitations foregrounded, so students build accurate conceptual understanding? Post your analogy, your three-criterion evaluation, and a two-sentence script for how you would address its limitations with students.

For AT2 Part A, you need to select a constructivist pedagogical approach and make it visible in your unit design. The 5E model (Engage–Explore–Explain–Elaborate–Evaluate), Predict-Observe-Explain (POE), and the Teaching-With-Analogies (TWA) model are three options. Choose one approach and identify a specific five-lesson sequence from any 7–10 strand where you could make each phase of the model recognisable in the lesson activities. Post your approach, the five-lesson sketch, and the phase label for each lesson. Which phase is hardest to design well, and why?
7.synthesisTemplate: Thanks for your Topic 8 posts — the content knowledge audit is the most personally honest piece of work in the unit, and it shows in the quality of reflection this week. Patterns worth noting: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most audits identified genuine gaps, but the gap-closure plans sometimes stayed at the category level — 'read more about genetics' rather than a specific resource, course, or person]. This matters for AT1 Part C and AT2 Part D: the rubric rewards naming a specific source and justifying why it is right for your strand and context, not naming a type of source. One thing to carry into your assessments: [FOCUS — e.g. the difference between 'ASTA' as a source and 'the ASTA Chemistry journal article on misconceptions about covalent bonding' — specificity is the point]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].

8.title: Developing Your Science Content Knowledge

announcementBody.intro: In our final teaching week, we return to a foundational professional responsibility: the ongoing development of your own science content knowledge. Strong content knowledge is not just a starting point — it is something you actively build and deepen throughout your teaching career.
announcementBody.focus: Topic 8 focuses on content knowledge development as a professional discipline. You will conduct a systematic audit of your content knowledge against your Stage 6 syllabus, identify specific gaps and priorities, and build a realistic plan for ongoing learning — including professional associations, subject-matter resources, and peer networks that support science content development.

8.liveSessionFocus: Session 8 covers Topics 7 and 8 together. We will examine analogies and content knowledge development as complementary professional skills, and conclude the teaching program by looking ahead to the professional learning habits that will sustain your practice.
liveSessionTasks: Complete a content knowledge audit for your primary Stage 6 teaching area: rate your confidence across the major content sections and identify your two or three priority gaps.
For each priority gap, identify at least one concrete resource or action you will take to address it — a specific book, online course, professional association, or peer with relevant expertise.
Come prepared to briefly share your audit findings and your plan — and to give and receive constructive feedback with peers.
8.orientationNote: Topic 8 is the direct preparation for AT1 Part C and AT2 Part D — both reflection tasks. A common mistake is treating these as summaries of what you learned. The rubric rewards something more specific: identifying a named external professional learning source (such as a NESA resource, ASTA, a professional association in your discipline, or a community representative) and explaining concretely why it is relevant to your teaching context and chosen strand. AT2 Part D also asks you to evaluate one aspect of your unit program with evidence. Use this week to audit your own content knowledge honestly and identify specific gaps — that audit is the raw material for a reflection that earns marks.
forumPrompts: Conduct a content knowledge audit for the strand you have chosen for AT1 or AT2. Rate your confidence across the main content areas of that strand on a simple three-point scale (strong / developing / gap). For your two most significant gaps, identify: (a) one specific professional learning resource or action that would address it — a named book, online course, ASTA workshop, university contact, or community expert; (b) why that resource is the right one for your specific gap and teaching context, not just a general reference. Post your audit summary and your two gap-closure plans. What would you need to be confident enough to teach that content well?

AT2 Part D asks you to 'evaluate one aspect of your unit program with evidence and name an external professional or community source that supported your design.' Practise this now: identify one specific decision you made in a draft of your AT2 unit program (or a program you have seen on placement), evaluate whether it worked or would work using a piece of evidence (student data, feedback, a peer review, or a cited research finding), and name one external professional or community source you consulted or would consult to strengthen that aspect. Post your decision, your evidence-based evaluation, and your named source. Why does naming a source matter — what does it demonstrate that a general reference does not?
8.synthesisTemplate: Thanks for your Topic 8 posts — the content knowledge audit is the most personally honest piece of work in the unit, and it shows in the quality of reflection this week. Patterns worth noting: [OBSERVATION — e.g. most audits identified genuine gaps, but the gap-closure plans sometimes stayed at the category level — 'read more about genetics' rather than a specific resource, course, or person]. This matters for AT1 Part C and AT2 Part D: the rubric rewards naming a specific source and justifying why it is right for your strand and context, not naming a type of source. One thing to carry into your assessments: [FOCUS — e.g. the difference between 'ASTA' as a source and 'the ASTA Chemistry journal article on misconceptions about covalent bonding' — specificity is the point]. See you [DAY] at [TIME].
